plainMeaning: This verse simply names eight more great souls — Jambūsvāmī, Vaṅkacūla, Gajasukumāla, Avantisukumāla, Dhanna, Ilāputra, Cilātiputra and the monk Bāhu — whose courageous, self-mastered lives we call to mind and honour.
recitation: jambūpahu vaṅkacūlo, gayasukumālo avantisukumālo, dhanno ilāiputto, cilāiputto a bāhumuṇi

wordByWord.etymology: jambū (personal name) + pahu < Skt. prabhu, 'lord, master'; an honorific 'Jambū the Lord'.
wordByWord.notes: Jambūsvāmī, disciple of Sudharmasvāmī, traditionally the last kevalī of this avasarpiṇī; renounced on his wedding night despite eight brides.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit proper name; nom. sg. -o (< Skt. -aḥ). Lit. 'crooked-crest' (vaṅka 'bent' + cūlā 'crest').
wordByWord.notes: A prince-turned-bandit who, by keeping four small vows received from a muni, was preserved from ruin — the verse's lone lay exemplar of partial vows.

wordByWord.etymology: gaya < Skt. gaja 'elephant' + sukumāla 'tender, delicate'; 'tender as a young elephant'. Nom. sg. -o.
wordByWord.notes: Younger kinsman of Kṛṣṇa, ordained by Neminātha; endured burning embers heaped on his head by Somila and reached liberation that very night.

wordByWord.etymology: avanti (the city/region of Avantī, Ujjayinī) + sukumāla 'delicate one'; 'the delicate one of Avantī'. Nom. sg. -o.
wordByWord.notes: A pampered nobleman of Ujjayinī who, on hearing the Nalinīgulma chapter, renounced and stood in kāyotsarga while a she-jackal and her cubs devoured his body, dying in unbroken equanimity.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dhanna < Skt. dhanya 'fortunate, blessed'; here a personal name. Nom. sg. -o.
wordByWord.notes: Dhanna Anagāra, praised by Mahāvīra as foremost among his ascetics in austerity (tapas); identification with Dhanna Sārthavāha varies by recension.

wordByWord.etymology: ilā (mother's name) + putta < Skt. putra 'son'; 'son of Ilā'. Nom. sg. -o; doubled -tt- is regular Prakrit assimilation of -tr-.
wordByWord.notes: Ilāciputra/Ilāputra: infatuated with an acrobat, he became a pole-dancer and won kevalajñāna atop the bamboo pole when a flash of detachment arose.

wordByWord.etymology: cilāti/cilāya (mother's name) + putta < Skt. putra 'son'; 'son of Cilātī'. Nom. sg. -o.
wordByWord.notes: A robber who, given the three words upaśama–viveka–saṁvara by a muni, stood motionless in meditation while ants pierced his body to the bone, dying serenely.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit a < Skt. ca, the connective 'and'; metrically reduced enclitic.
wordByWord.notes: Conjoins the final name to the list; a recurring filler that also fits the gāthā metre.

wordByWord.etymology: bāhu (personal name; lit. 'arm') + muṇi < Skt. muni 'sage, ascetic'; 'the sage Bāhu'.
wordByWord.notes: Bāhu muni, remembered for exemplary vaiyāvṛtya (selfless service to the saṅgha); see notes on identification.

literalTranslation: Lord Jambū, Vaṅkacūla, Gajasukumāla, Avantisukumāla, Dhanna, Ilāputra, Cilātiputra and Bāhu-muni.
idiomaticTranslation: Jambū the Lord, and Vaṅkacūla; Gajasukumāla and Avantisukumāla; Dhanna the blessed, Ilāputra, and Cilātiputra, and the sage Bāhu — these too we remember.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the preceding verse closed a cluster of court-and-kingdom figures, this gāthā gathers eight souls bound by one thread: each met a crushing ordeal (upasarga) or temptation and answered it with unbroken equanimity. The roll is deliberately varied. Jambū conquers desire — renouncing on his wedding night amid eight brides, the last to win omniscience in our age. Vaṅkacūla, the verse's single non-monastic, shows that even four small vows, faithfully kept, can save a man the world had given up for lost. Then come the great endurances of the body: Gajasukumāla, tender as his name, stands firm under embers piled on his shaved head and is liberated the same night; Avantisukumāla lets a she-jackal and her cubs feed on him without a flinch of aversion; Cilātiputra, once a murderer, holds the three words upaśama–viveka–saṁvara in his heart while ants bore through his flesh. Dhanna embodies voluntary austerity rather than imposed pain — foremost in tapas among Mahāvīra's monks. Ilāputra reverses the pattern: not torment but a sudden inward turning, mid-performance atop the bamboo pole, ripens into kevalajñāna. Bāhu-muni completes the set with the quiet heroism of service (vaiyāvṛtya). Read together, the eight map the whole terrain of spiritual courage — over lust, over fear, over the body, over a violent past, over fame — so that the reciter, naming them, rehearses every kind of victory the path may ask.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse is a compact lesson in parīṣaha-jaya and upasarga-titikṣā: suffering met without rāga or dveṣa becomes sakāma-nirjarā, the deliberate shedding of karma, and several here reach the goal within the very ordeal. Vaṅkacūla illustrates the protective power of even partial restraint (deśavirati / aṇuvrata), a pointed encouragement to the lay reciter, while Cilātiputra's three-word formula compresses Jain praxis itself — calming the passions (upaśama), discernment (viveka), and stopping karmic influx (saṁvara). Dhanna anchors tapas as a pillar, and Bāhu-muni names vaiyāvṛtya, service, as itself a high meritorious act. The common denominator is samādhi sustained to the last breath.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Naming these eight, the reciter is asked which ordeal is his own today — craving, fear, bodily discomfort, a shameful past, or pride — and is shown a face of equanimity for each. Vaṅkacūla's example is especially close to the householder: keep even a few vows scrupulously, and they will guard you when willpower fails.

crossReferences.text: Anuttaraupapātika-daśā (Dhanna Anagāra praised foremost in tapas)
Āvaśyaka-niryukti / cūrṇi exemplar narratives (Ilāputra, Cilātiputra, Vaṅkacūla)

sources.consultedFor: The wedding-night renunciation episode and Jambūsvāmī's place in the Śvetāmbara tradition.

sources.consultedFor: Spelling, Sanskrit equivalence (Cilātiputra) and lexical attestation of the name.

notes: Names are read per the book's verbatim Prakrit orthography; nominative -o endings and doubled -tt- (putto) are regular Māhārāṣṭrī/Ardhamāgadhī forms, not errors. 'Ilāiputto' appears as Ilāputra/Ilāciputra across recensions; both denote the same pole-dancer who attained kevalajñāna. 'Bāhumuṇi' is identified here as the muni Bāhu celebrated for vaiyāvṛtya (service); some traditions instead gloss it as Bāhu-muni of the Bāhu–Subāhu (earlier-birth) narratives. Because the source book does not gloss the individual names, these story-identifications are drawn from the wider kathā/Āgama tradition and should be treated as the standard but not book-attested reading; the secure data here is the lexical word-by-word, not the biographies. Dhanna's exact identity (Dhanna Anagāra vs. Dhanna Sārthavāha) varies by recension.
